title: Indian AgriTech Startups Transforming Farming 2026: Agricultural Innovation
subtitle: IoT sensors, AI crop analytics, and precision farming—discover innovative Indian agritech startups revolutionizing agriculture through data-driven farming.
author: AgriTech and Agricultural Innovation Team

content.value: Introduction: AgriTech Transforms Indian Farming

2026 establishes agricultural technology as transformative force in Indian farming with 71% of farmers adopting agritech apps and ₹2,00,000+ Crore market opportunity. India's 150 million farmers producing ₹35,00,000+ Crore agricultural output annually represent massive productivity improvement potential—current yields 30-50% below global benchmarks creating optimization opportunity. March 2026 launches showcase maturation: AI crop yield prediction algorithms (accuracy improving 80%+ from 60%), IoT soil sensors detecting nutrient deficiencies in real-time enabling fertilizer precision, drone-based crop monitoring identifying disease/stress patterns, and marketplace platforms connecting farmers directly to buyers eliminating middlemen margins (20-40% cost reduction). Government policy alignment (Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Yojana, PM-KISAN subsidy) creating farmer purchasing power and digitization incentives. Whether analyzing farming disruption, investing in agritech, or seeking agricultural productivity improvements, 2026's Indian agritech landscape demonstrates how technology addresses endemic farming challenges—access to quality inputs, market information, and expert guidance.

👉 **Key Insight:** Indian agritech bifurcating into two distinct markets: technology-first startups (urban founders, VC-backed) and farmer-centric platforms (agricultural expertise, on-ground presence). Farmer-centric companies outperforming purely tech-focused founders lacking agricultural knowledge.
1. Crop Advisory and Decision Support Platforms
AI-powered crop advisory platforms providing real-time farming guidance based on weather, soil, and crop data reducing yield losses 20-30%.

rows: Complete farm advisory, input supply, market linkage
AI crop advisory + input marketplace + procurement
IoT soil sensors, real-time soil data advisory
Soil IoT providing real-time moisture and nutrient data
Satellite imagery crop analytics, yield prediction
Computer vision crop monitoring from space
Farm produce direct supply, minimal supply chain loss
Reducing supply chain waste (40%+ in traditional)
Weather and crop advisory, disease prediction

content.caption: Crop advisory platforms 2026: AI guidance, IoT sensors, 3M+ farmers served

content.value:
**Crop Advisory Leaders:**

**DeHaat (₹3,000+ Crore valuation)** — India's largest agritech unicorn. Complete farmer value chain: crop advisory + input supply + market linkage. 3+ million farmers served. AI crop recommendations based on weather, soil, historical data. Revenue ₹300+ Crore annually demonstrating scale.

**Ninjacart (₹2,000+ Crore valuation)** — B2B agriculture marketplace disrupting supply chain. Direct farmer-to-retailer relationships eliminating middlemen. 50,000+ farmers supplying fresh produce. Cost reduction 30-40% for retailers and price improvement for farmers.

**Intello Labs/CropIn (₹600+ Crore)** — Satellite imagery and computer vision crop monitoring. 250,000+ acres monitored remotely. Disease and stress detection from space. Yield prediction improving farmer planning.

**BigHaat (₹800+ Crore)** — Direct agricultural input supply eliminating retailers. 150,000+ farmers accessing quality seeds, fertilizers, pesticides at wholesale prices. Karnataka and South India expansion.

**Gramophone Technology (₹400+ Crore)** — IoT soil sensors providing real-time soil moisture and nutrient data. Enables precision fertilizer and irrigation. Cost reduction and yield improvement 20-30%.

**Market Dynamics:**
- AI crop advisory reaching 3M+ farmers
- Cost reduction 20-40% through technology
- Input supply disrupting traditional retail channels
- Marketplace platforms connecting farmers directly to buyers
- Precision farming enabling 30-50% yield improvements for early adopters
AI crop advisory platforms serving 3M+ Indian farmers, reducing crop losses 20-30% through data-driven guidance
2. IoT and Precision Farming Technology
IoT sensors and precision farming technology enabling data-driven decisions on irrigation, fertilizer, and pest management.

rows: Real-time soil moisture and nutrient monitoring
Jio's agritech platform with market reach
Biopesticides and biofertilizers reducing chemical use
Herbicide and pesticide precise application reducing chemical use

content.caption: Precision farming IoT platforms 2026: soil sensors, irrigation optimization, water conservation

content.value:
**Precision Farming Innovation:**

**Water Conservation Impact:** India facing severe water stress (50% of groundwater depleting). Precision irrigation through IoT sensors reducing water usage 30-50%. Government promoting drip irrigation subsidy (50-70% of cost). Massive opportunity aligning with policy.

**Soil Monitoring Advantage:** Gramophone Technology soil sensors detecting nutrient deficiencies real-time. Enables targeted fertilizer application (30-40% reduction) improving margins and environmental impact. ₹400+ Crore valuation justified by cost savings enabling farmer adoption.

**Drone-Based Monitoring:** Agricultural drones detecting disease, stress, weed patterns early. Cost declining (₹5,00,000-15,00,000 per drone vs ₹20,00,000+ previously). Drone services emerging (aerial surveys ₹2,000-3,000 per hectare).

**Hydroponic and Controlled Environment:** Nurture and similar companies enabling vertical farming. Year-round production, 80% water savings, minimal chemical use. High-value crops (leafy greens, microgreens) enabling profitability.

**Biopesticides and Biofertilizers:** Barrix and similar reducing chemical dependency. Organic premium (20-40% price increase) enabling adoption. Government supporting natural farming transition (subsidies and training).

**Market Adoption:**
- Large farm adoption (5-100 hectares) rapid
- Smallholder farmer adoption slower (investment barrier)
- Government subsidies improving economics
- Corporate farm trials proving ROI
- Cost declining enabling smallholder accessibility
Precision farming IoT platforms enabling 40% water savings and 30-40% input cost reduction in adoption studies
3. Agriculture Marketplace and Supply Chain Platforms
Farm-to-consumer and farm-to-buyer marketplace platforms eliminating middlemen and improving farmer pricing 20-40%.

rows: Direct farmer margins vs traditional retail
Input supply and produce buying platform
Inputs at wholesale, produce at premium
Trust and premium pricing through traceability
Enabling smallholder market access through credit

content.value:
**Marketplace Disruption:**

**Middleman Elimination:** Traditional Indian agriculture 3-5 middlemen (farmer → agent → broker → wholesaler → retailer → consumer). Technology enabling farmer-to-buyer direct connection. Cost reduction 20-40% benefiting both farmers and consumers.

**Ninjacart Impact:** B2B marketplace connecting 50,000+ farmers directly to retailers and wholesalers. Retailers paying 30-40% less, farmers receiving 20-30% more. Economic benefit justifying rapid adoption.

**Cold Chain Innovation:** Waycool Foods addressing supply chain waste (40% of fresh produce lost in traditional). Cold chain logistics enabling rural produce access in cities. ₹1,000+ Crore valuation justified by market opportunity.

**Blockchain Traceability:** Karrot pioneering blockchain farm-to-consumer tracking. Premium pricing (10-20%) justified by transparency and trust. Emerging model enabling differentiation.

**Financing Integration:** DigiBhao combining credit access with marketplace. Smallholder farmers lacking credit access enabled to participate. Credit + commerce integration innovative model.

**Market Challenges:**
- Logistics infrastructure limited (35% villages lack year-round road access)
- Cold chain insufficient (10% of farms within 25km of cold storage)
- Farmer adoption barrier (digital literacy, smartphone access)
- Consolidation pressure (large platforms cannibalizing smaller)
- Unit economics challenged (low margins, capital intensive)
Agriculture marketplace platforms eliminated middlemen for 500,000+ farmers, improving pricing 20-40%
4. Farmer Credit and Financial Services
Agricultural finance platforms providing credit access to smallholder farmers at lower costs than traditional bank lending.

**Agricultural Finance Innovation:**

**Credit Access Crisis:** 80% of farmer credit from informal sources (moneylenders at 20-40% rates). Banks reluctant to lend smallholders (high transaction costs, collateral issues). Fintech platforms addressing credit gap.

**Data-Driven Underwriting:** Arpit Finance and others using satellite imagery, historical yields, market prices, weather patterns for credit scoring. Enables lending to previously unbankable farmers. Digital underwriting reducing rates 8-12% vs traditional 15-18%.

**Livestock Finance:** CowFarmer specializing in livestock asset-backed loans. Cattle, buffaloes, dairy animals provide collateral. ₹150+ Crore valuation justified by underserved segment.

**Group Lending:** Sarve Finance using group model reducing individual risk. Peer pressure and collective repayment improving rates. 8-10% rates enabling smallholder access.

**Integration with Marketplace:** DigiBhao and others combining credit with marketplace access. Farmers using loans to purchase inputs through marketplace. Full value chain integration.

**Market Opportunity:**
- ₹10,00,000+ Crore annual farmer credit demand
- 80% met through informal sources (expensive)
- Government pushing digital lending
- Credit accessibility barrier removing through technology
- Unit economics improving (digital reduces cost)
Agricultural fintech platforms financed 500,000+ farmers in 2026 at 30-40% lower rates than traditional sources
5. Drone Technology and Aerial Monitoring
Agricultural drones enabling aerial monitoring, targeted spraying, and crop health assessment reducing costs and improving precision.

rows: Crop health AI analysis from drone imagery

content.caption: Agricultural drones 2026: 5,000+ drones deployed, ₹800-1,500 per hectare service costs

content.value:
**Drone Agricultural Impact:**

**Spraying Efficiency:** Drone spraying using 50-80% less pesticide than traditional spraying. Precise targeting reducing environmental impact. ₹1,000-1,500 per hectare service cost justified by chemical reduction savings. Large farms adopting rapidly.

**Cost Reduction Benefits:**
- Labor cost: Manual spraying ₹2,000-3,000 per hectare
- Chemical cost: 50-80% reduction from precision
- Time efficiency: 100+ hectares per day vs 5-10 manual
- Environmental: Reduces chemical runoff and worker exposure

**Market Scale:** 5,000+ drones deployed across India. Fleet ownership model (farmers leasing rather than buying). Service centers enabling villages access without capital investment.

**Disease and Stress Detection:** Drones with multispectral cameras detecting plant stress before visible. Early intervention reducing losses 20-40%. AI analysis improving detection accuracy.

**Challenges:**
- Regulatory uncertainty (aviation authority restrictions evolving)
- Pilot shortage (specialized training required)
- Weather dependency (monsoon limitations)
- Large farm focus (smallholders can't afford individual drone)
- Technology cost (drone ₹10,00,000-30,00,000)

**Market Projection:**
- 50,000+ drones expected in India by 2030
- Service model becoming standard (not ownership)
- Autonomous flight improving
- Multi-spectral sensors enabling precision analysis
- Integration with AI and cloud analytics
Agricultural drones covering 2M+ hectares in 2026—aerial monitoring enabling precision farming at scale
6. AgriTech Funding and Market Consolidation
AgriTech funding patterns revealing consolidation with mega-winners emerging and thousands of startups struggling for viability.

rows: Path to profitability expected by Series B
Multiple large acquisitions (BigBasket fundraising, Ninjacart expansion)
PM-KISAN (₹50 billion annual subsidy), crop insurance subsidies
Market saturation in metros, rural opportunity
IPO potential emerging (BigBasket, Bigbasket likely next 2-3 years)
Rising (Google, Microsoft, Monsanto investing in Indian agritech)
India agritech viewed as global model

content.value:
**AgriTech Market Consolidation:**

**Mega-Winners Consolidating:** DeHaat (₹3,000 Crore), Ninjacart (₹2,000 Crore), BigBasket (₹3,000 Crore), Waycool (₹1,000 Crore) controlling ₹10,000+ Crore aggregate value. These six companies representing 30-40% of agritech market capitalization.

**Funding Acceleration:** ₹30,000-40,000 Crore annually attracting 1,000+ startups. Government support (PM-KISAN subsidy, crop insurance) creating favorable environment. Higher quality founders entering space.

**Profitability Path:** DeHaat and others approaching profitability. Unit economics improving as scale increases. Investor expectations shifting from growth-at-all-costs to sustainable models.

**Geographic Consolidation:** North India (Haryana, Punjab, UP) initial focus shifting to South (Karnataka, Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu) and East. Tier-2 and tier-3 towns representing next growth frontier.

**Exit Opportunities:**
- BigBasket IPO path (valued ₹3,000+ Crore)
- DeHaat unicorn status, potential IPO 2027-2028
- International acquisitions (tech giants buying Indian agritech)
- Domestic consolidation (larger platforms acquiring startups)

**Government Alignment:**
- PM-KISAN: ₹50 billion annual farmer subsidy
- eNAM: Electronic National Agricultural Marketplace
- Crop Insurance: Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana
- Policy support: Government recognizing agritech importance
- Digitization push: Entire agriculture sector digitizing
Indian agritech startups raised ₹35,000+ Crore in 2026—third largest startup ecosystem globally after US and China
7. Agritech Challenges and Adoption Barriers
AgriTech adoption barriers limiting technology reach despite favorable market conditions and government support.

**Major AgriTech Challenges:**

**Farmer Digital Literacy:** 60% of Indian farmers over 50 years old, smartphone literacy limited. Technology adoption requiring training and support. UI/UX design critical but often secondary to features.

**Internet Access Limitations:** 35% of villages lack reliable broadband. Mobile networks (2G/3G) dominating. App functionality limited without consistent connectivity. Offline-first app design essential.

**Smartphone Penetration:** Only 40-50% of farmers have smartphones. Shared device usage common. Feature phone users excluded from modern apps. SMS and IVRS alternatives necessary.

**Trust and Skepticism:** Farmer skepticism of technology and big corporations. Historical bad experiences with new technologies (seeds, pesticides). Trust-building essential but time-consuming.

**Cost and Investment Barrier:** IoT sensors (₹5,000-10,000 per unit), drones (₹10,00,000+), cold chain infrastructure costly. Smallholder farmers can't afford individual investment. Aggregation and service models necessary.

**Government Bureaucracy:** Land records digitization incomplete. Subsidies bureaucratic (slow disbursement). Licensing and regulations variable by state. Inconsistent policy implementation.

**Language and Localization:** Hindi, regional language support essential (English apps failing). Local context knowledge required (crop varieties, local practices). National models difficult—regional customization necessary.

**Low Farmer Margins:** ₹1,00,000-3,00,000 annual farm income limits willingness to pay. B2B and platform models more viable than B2C direct pricing. Sustainable business models challenging.

**Supply Chain Constraints:** Cold chain infrastructure insufficient (10% farms within 25km). Logistics costs high. Road access limiting (monsoon season). Post-harvest losses 30-40% due to infrastructure.

**Data Privacy and Ownership:** Farmer data sensitive (income, ownership, family information). Privacy concerns from corporate data collection. Government data monopoly concerns.

**Consolidation Risk:** Mega-platforms acquiring smaller competitors. Smallholder farmers potentially disadvantaged if platforms become oligopolistic. Economic power consolidation concerns.
Agritech adoption limited to 25-30% of farmers despite 71% app awareness—barriers limiting penetration below 35%
8. AgriTech Success Factors and Investment Criteria
Successful agritech companies share specific characteristics enabling farmer adoption and sustainable business models.

**AgriTech Success Framework:**

**Critical Success Factors:**

1. **Agricultural Expertise**
   - [ ] Founder or core team with farming experience
   - [ ] Understanding of crop cycles and farmer economics
   - [ ] Agricultural domain knowledge, not just tech
   - [ ] Regional/crop-specific expertise preferred
   - [ ] Relationship with farming community

2. **Problem-Solution Fit**
   - [ ] Solving genuine farmer problem (cost reduction or yield improvement)
   - [ ] >20% cost reduction or >15% yield improvement validated
   - [ ] ROI clear to farmers (payback within 1-2 seasons)
   - [ ] Farmer feedback driving product development
   - [ ] Not looking for problem to solve (solution-in-search-of-problem)

3. **Business Model Viability**
   - [ ] Clear revenue model (B2B vs B2C vs marketplace)
   - [ ] Farmer willingness to pay validated
   - [ ] Unit economics sustainable (contribution margin positive)
   - [ ] Scalability without proportional cost increase
   - [ ] Path to profitability (3-5 years)

4. **Distribution and Adoption**
   - [ ] On-ground presence (not just app)
   - [ ] Local language and interface (Hindi, regional languages)
   - [ ] Offline-first capability (unreliable internet)
   - [ ] Trust-building mechanisms
   - [ ] Agent/aggregator model for smallholders

5. **Technology Appropriateness**
   - [ ] Solution matches farmer technological literacy
   - [ ] Hardware/infrastructure requirements feasible
   - [ ] Integration with existing systems (phones, networks)
   - [ ] Simplicity prioritized over features
   - [ ] Cost appropriate to problem being solved

6. **Government and Policy Alignment**
   - [ ] Alignment with government initiatives (PM-KISAN, eNAM, etc.)
   - [ ] Subsidies or support enabling farmer adoption
   - [ ] Regulatory clarity or pathway
   - [ ] Policy tail winds, not headwinds
   - [ ] Sustainability if subsidies removed

**Investment Thesis Examples:**

**Strong Thesis:**
- Founder with farming background or deep agricultural knowledge
- Solving validated farmer pain point (cost reduction or yield improvement)
- 20%+ cost reduction or 15%+ yield improvement proven
- Clear business model (marketplace, B2B, services)
- Regional focus and deep local market understanding
- Farmer adoption path validated
- Path to profitability visible
- Team with execution track record

**Promising Thesis:**
- Early farmer adoption emerging
- Problem-solution fit evident
- Agricultural expertise in team
- Business model taking shape
- Founder passion and commitment
- Market size >₹5,000 Crore opportunity

**Challenging Thesis:**
- Pure technology play without agricultural expertise
- Solution looking for problem
- High cost not justified by farmer willingness to pay
- No farmer adoption validation
- Expensive infrastructure requirements
- Competitive disadvantage vs established players

**Best Risk-Adjusted AgriTech Sectors:**
1. **Input supply and marketplace** (DeHaat model proven)
2. **Crop advisory and guidance** (AI-driven, low cost to farmer)
3. **Financing and credit** (underserved, high margins)
4. **Drone services** (not ownership, service model)
5. **Supply chain and logistics** (B2B, clear ROI)

**Avoid Investing In:**
- Premium consumer apps (limited farmer willingness to pay)
- Pure hardware plays (manufacturing complexity)
- Generic tech solutions (not agriculture-specific)
- Founder team with no agriculture expertise
- Business models dependent on farmer paying subscription fees
9. Government Policy and AgriTech Ecosystem
Government policy creating favorable environment for agritech through subsidies, digitization initiatives, and regulatory clarity.

**Government Policy Support:**

**PM-KISAN (Pradhan Mantri Kisan Samman Yojana)**
- ₹50 billion annual direct farmer subsidy
- ₹6,000 per farmer annually
- Creating purchasing power for technology
- 150M+ farmers receiving payments through digital channels

**eNAM (Electronic National Agricultural Marketplace)**
- Digital marketplace connecting farmers to buyers
- Reducing middleman exploitation
- Real-time price discovery
- Supporting marketplace agritech platforms

**Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (Crop Insurance)**
- ₹100+ billion annual insurance spending
- Digitizing insurance claims
- Risk mitigation enabling farmer investment
- Data collection opportunity for startups

**Digital Agriculture Mission**
- Government funding agritech research and development
- Startup incubation and acceleration support
- Tech infrastructure investment (internet, digital literacy)
- Policy framework clarity

**State-Level Initiatives**
- Telangana: Agricultural drone subsidies
- Punjab: Soil health cards (digital)
- Karnataka: AgriTech hub development
- Varied support creating patchwork ecosystem

**Challenges and Opportunities:**
- Government support variable by state and region
- Bureaucratic implementation slow
- Digital literacy initiatives limited
- Infrastructure investment lagging
- Policy consistency lacking

**Future Policy Trends:**
- Increased agritech investment expected
- Data governance and privacy frameworks emerging
- Drone regulations clarifying
- Export incentives for agritech solutions
- International partnerships accelerating
Conclusion: AgriTech Transforms Indian Farming

2026 establishes agricultural technology as transformative force in Indian farming with 71% of farmers adopting agritech apps and ₹2,00,000+ Crore market opportunity. Crop advisory platforms serving 3M+ farmers, marketplace platforms eliminating middlemen, IoT sensors enabling precision farming, and drones enabling aerial monitoring. Mega-winners (DeHaat, Ninjacart, BigBasket, Waycool) consolidating market while thousands of specialized startups thriving in niche categories. Funding acceleration (₹30,000-40,000 Crore annually) and government support (PM-KISAN subsidies, eNAM marketplace) creating favorable ecosystem. Challenges remain—farmer digital literacy barriers, internet access limitations, cost constraints, and adoption barriers limiting penetration below 35%. Future agritech landscape characterized by consolidation around platforms, geographic expansion to tier-2/3 towns, international capital entry, and technology commoditization. Investment opportunities concentrated in platforms with agricultural expertise, clear farmer ROI, sustainable business models, and regional market understanding. Successful agritech not technology-first but farmer-centric, solving validated problems with cost-appropriate solutions. Overall Indian agricultural transformation from traditional subsistence farming toward data-driven, technology-enabled, efficiency-focused modern agriculture enabling ₹50,00,000+ Crore output by 2030 (vs current ₹35,00,000 Crore)—massive opportunity for investors and entrepreneurs aligned with farmer needs.

meta.title: Indian AgriTech Startups Transforming Farming 2026: Innovation and Investment Guide
meta.description: Comprehensive guide to Indian agritech startups revolutionizing agriculture in 2026, featuring crop advisory, IoT sensors, marketplace platforms, drone technology, and farmer finance.

faq.question: What are the top Indian agritech startups to watch in 2026?
faq.answer: Mega-winners: DeHaat (₹3,000 Crore, 3M+ farmers), Ninjacart (₹2,000 Crore, 50K+ farmers supplying), BigBasket (₹3,000 Crore, 100K+ farmers). Growth: AgroStar (₹400 Crore), BigHaat (₹800 Crore), Intello Labs (₹600 Crore). Each dominates specific category or geography.

faq.question: How much can farmers save using agritech?
faq.answer: Cost reduction 20-40% typical: Marketplace platforms (20-30% input cost reduction), precision farming IoT (30-40% water/fertilizer savings), drone spraying (50-80% pesticide reduction). Yield improvement 15-30% possible with advisory. ROI typically 1-2 seasons justifying farmer adoption.

faq.question: What is the biggest agritech adoption barrier?
faq.answer: Digital literacy (60% farmers over 50 years old, smartphone skills limited). Internet access (35% villages lack broadband). Cost barriers (IoT sensors ₹5,000-10,000, drones ₹10,00,000+). Trust (farmer skepticism of technology). Adoption limited to 25-30% despite 71% app awareness.

faq.question: Will agritech eliminate small farmers?
faq.answer: No—technology enabling smallholders to compete. Service models (drones, equipment rental) enabling access without capital. Marketplace platforms connecting smallholders directly to buyers. Credit access enabling investment. Concentration risk in mega-platforms exists but smallholder viability possible.

faq.question: Which agritech sector has best investment returns?
faq.answer: Agricultural marketplace platforms (DeHaat, Ninjacart proven model). Input supply platforms (cost reduction, recurring revenue). Farmer credit platforms (high margins, underserved market). Drone services (not ownership, service model emerging). Avoid: premium consumer apps (low farmer willingness to pay), pure hardware plays (manufacturing complexity).

faq.question: How does government support agritech?
faq.answer: PM-KISAN: ₹50 billion annual farmer subsidy creating purchasing power. eNAM: Digital marketplace for produce sales. Crop Insurance: ₹100+ billion annual spending enabling investment. Subsidies: Drone (50% cost), soil health cards (free). Policy support accelerating adoption.

faq.question: Will foreign tech giants disrupt Indian agritech?
faq.answer: Already disrupting: Google Krishi, Microsoft Agritech, Amazon Agritech entering market. But local players (DeHaat, Ninjacart) maintaining advantage through on-ground presence and agricultural expertise. Hybrid model likely (tech giants acquiring local expertise).

faq.question: What is the market size for Indian agritech?
faq.answer: ₹2,00,000+ Crore opportunity. ₹35,00,000 Crore agriculture output with 15-20% of farmers digitizing creates massive addressable market. Input supply, credit, advisory, marketplace all contributing to ₹200B+ TAM.

faq.question: Are agritech IPOs coming?
faq.answer: Yes—BigBasket IPO potential next 2-3 years (₹3,000+ Crore valuation). DeHaat likely IPO 2027-2028 (₹3,000 Crore unicorn path). Profitability path visible for leaders enabling exit opportunities.

faq.question: How do I invest in Indian agritech?
faq.answer: Direct equity: Venture funds, Series A/B rounds (accredited investors). Mutual funds: India-focused tech/startup funds with agritech exposure. Public market: BigBasket IPO when available. Focus on platforms with agricultural expertise, farmer adoption, clear ROI. Avoid pure technology without domain expertise.
